meta.title: ECL Patterns: Extract, Conform, Load
meta.thesis: Pure EL (Extract-Load with zero transformation) is a practical myth. The moment data crosses between systems, conforming is unavoidable: type casting, metadata injection, null handling, charset encoding. ECL (Extract, Conform, Load) names this reality. The C is everything the data needs to survive the crossing and be usable on the other side. If it changes business logic, it belongs downstream — not here.
meta.audience: Data engineers building and operating batch extraction pipelines. Senior practitioners, not beginners. They know SQL, they've run pipelines, they've been burned. The book gives them frameworks to stop making the same mistakes.
meta.voice: Technical but human. Opinionated. Fast to scan. No filler. Like a senior engineer explaining to a peer over coffee.
meta.scope: Batch processing only. No CDC, no streaming, no event-driven.

design-notes.format: SVG output. Screen-first (SQL codeblocks need width), print as secondary.
design-notes.typesetting: Typst for final layout. Graphics are embedded as SVGs.
design-notes.existing-framework: _charts/ contains ecl-charts.js with ECL.segment(), ECL.timeline(), ECL.tableState(), and ECL.tradeoff() APIs. The current aesthetic is not final — these graphics may establish a new visual style.
design-notes.punctuation: Use -- (double hyphen) instead of em dash everywhere.

domain-model.description: All examples in the book use these fictional tables. Graphics that reference specific tables should use these names.

table.name: invoices
table.note: Open/closed document pattern. Open invoices get hard-deleted

framework-map.description: This is the meta-graphic — how all frameworks connect. Consider designing this as an overview/poster that orients the reader before diving into individual frameworks.

layer.name: governing-boundaries
layer.role: Constrain what the pipeline is allowed to do

layer.name: fundamental-tradeoff
layer.role: The axis every decision takes a position on

layer.name: structural-classifier
layer.role: Bifurcates every implementation into two paths

layer.name: design-philosophy
layer.role: Orders decisions from simple to complex

layer.name: decision-tools
layer.contains: statelessness-idempotency-matrix, load-strategy-spectrum, schema-evolution-policy, open-closed-documents, managed-frozen-zones, source-sensitivity-tiers

layer.name: operational-models
layer.contains: hot-warm-cold-freshness, four-layers-observability, reconciliation-escalation, severity-calibration, scheduling-maturity, data-contract-taxonomy, four-execution-points, morning-routine-triage, backfill-type-taxonomy
layer.role: How to run and monitor the pipeline in production

framework.name: conforming-vs-transforming
framework.idea: Every operation during the crossing is either conforming (survive the trip) or transforming (change business meaning). Only conforming belongs in ECL.
framework.decision-test: If a join adds a column the business cares about -- transforming. If it adds a column only the pipeline cares about (_cursor_at) -- conforming.

framework.name: hard-rules-soft-rules
framework.idea: Hard rules are enforced by the database (constraints, indexes). Soft rules are business expectations with no enforcement. Your pipeline must survive soft rules being wrong.
framework.discovery: Query information_schema.table_constraints. Everything absent from that output is soft.
framework.response: When a soft rule breaks: load the data, surface the violation, don't fix it in the pipeline.

framework.name: purity-vs-freshness
framework.idea: Full replace maximizes purity (exact clone, no drift) but caps freshness. Incremental maximizes freshness but accumulates purity debt until the next full reset. Every pipeline decision takes a position on this spectrum.

axes.axis: Destination exactly matches source at a point in time. No drift, no missed rows, no accumulated damage.
How recently the destination reflects the source.

framework.decision-tree: Full scan fits window? -> Reliable cursor? -> Hard deletes? -> Freshness SLA? Each "no" pushes toward incremental and away from purity.

framework.name: corridors
framework.used-by: Every pattern chapter via "By Corridor" sections
framework.idea: The source-destination type pairing changes every implementation decision. Same extraction pattern, different tradeoffs per corridor.

corridor.name: transactional-to-columnar
corridor.property: Wide -- many source types have no direct equivalent
Expensive -- MERGE is the costliest DML
Pay per bytes scanned / slots consumed

corridor.name: transactional-to-transactional
corridor.property: Cheap -- MERGE / INSERT ON CONFLICT is native

framework.name: earn-complexity
framework.idea: Start with the simplest approach (full replace). Add complexity only when a concrete limitation forces it. Every step up requires justification.

progression.step: Default. Stateless, idempotent, pure. No cursor, no state, no drift.

step.label: Scoped full replace
step.trigger: Full table too big for the schedule window

progression.step: Replace a date range (fiscal year, rolling window) instead of the whole table.

step.label: Rolling window replace
step.trigger: Scope boundary is metadata-driven, not calendar-driven

progression.step: Replace based on _extracted_at or activity signal rather than a fixed date.

step.label: Cursor-based incremental
step.trigger: Table genuinely too large to full-replace at required cadence

progression.step: Track what changed since last run. Requires reliable cursor. Accumulates purity debt.

step.label: Cursor + delete detection + periodic full reset

progression.step: Maximum complexity. Incremental for changes, separate pass for deletes, periodic full replace to reset drift. "The upper bound of what's reasonable."

framework.principle: Each step is triggered by a concrete "no" in the 0108 decision tree. If you can't name the trigger, you haven't earned the complexity.

framework.name: statelessness-idempotency-matrix
framework.idea: Two independent properties of a pipeline run. The goal is stateless AND idempotent. Full replace gets both for free. Every other approach trades one or both away.

matrix.quadrant: Stateless window + MERGE. Can run on a fresh machine, running twice produces the same result.
Cursor-based + MERGE. Needs prior state (last cursor value), but retries are safe.
Stateless window + raw INSERT. No state needed, but retries create duplicates.
Cursor-based + raw INSERT. Needs state AND retries create duplicates.

framework.note: Full replace is stateless + idempotent by construction -- no matrix needed.

framework.name: managed-frozen-zones
framework.idea: Data in the destination is partitioned into two zones with different replacement semantics. The managed zone gets replaced regularly. The frozen zone was loaded once and is never touched again.

zones.zone: Full-replace semantics apply. Idempotent reloads. Every run rebuilds this zone.
Loaded via one-time backfill. Immutable in the destination. Only touched during explicit re-backfill.

boundary-types.type: A specific date chosen at setup time
Last N days / last N months (boundary moves)

framework.name: open-closed-documents
framework.idea: A document's lifecycle state (mutable draft vs. immutable posted) drives the extraction strategy. Open documents need full re-extraction every run. Closed documents are safe to extract once and ignore.
framework.visualization-type: lifecycle state diagram or split pipeline flow

states.state: Lines change, statuses shift, amounts adjust. Re-extract the full set every run. Example: draft invoices, pending orders.

Frozen after posting. In many jurisdictions, modifying a closed invoice is illegal -- the rare case where a soft rule is backed by a hard rule (the law). Extract only recently closed documents via cursor.

framework.extraction-split: Open documents: full replace (small, bounded set). Closed documents: cursor-based incremental (large, growing set, immutable).

framework.name: hot-warm-cold-freshness
framework.idea: Not every row needs the same refresh cadence. Three tiers: hot for freshness (tolerates impurity), warm for catching gaps, cold for purity resets. Tier determines cadence. Method (FR vs incremental) is a separate decision based on table size and frequency.
framework.visualization-type: three-band horizontal timeline or stacked zones

tier.name: hot
tier.method: incremental when needed, FR when table is small enough

tiers.tier: Actively changing data: today's orders, open invoices, recent events. Tolerates imprecision -- warm catches what hot misses.

tier.name: warm
tier.method: FR of warm window, or wide-lag incremental

tiers.tier: Current month/quarter. Catches late cursor updates, backdated transactions, documents that changed without updating their updated_at.
Historical data: prior years, closed fiscal periods. The cleanup pass. Resets accumulated drift from hot and warm.

framework.lag-window: Overlap between tiers. 7 days for clean systems, 30 for messy ERPs. Empirical: start at 7, widen if the cold tier catches rows warm missed.
framework.key-insight: Being in the hot tier does not mean incremental. A small table can full-replace every run even at high frequency. Tier = cadence. Method = separate decision.

framework.name: four-layers-observability
framework.idea: Observability breaks into four layers, each covering a different failure mode. You don't need all four on day one -- start with 1 and 2.
framework.visualization-type: stacked layers or concentric rings (inner = start here)

layers.layer: Did the pipeline run? Did it succeed? How long did it take? Orchestrator-native. Every pipeline has this.
What did the pipeline produce? Row counts, freshness, schema fingerprints. The gap the health table (0602) fills.
Query duration at source, timeout frequency, load impact measurement. Earns its place as pipeline count grows.
Cost per operation, destination row count, reconciliation delta. The cost monitoring layer (0603).

framework.name: reconciliation-escalation
framework.idea: Three levels of verification, ordered cheap to expensive. Start cheap, escalate only when the cheaper level signals a problem.
framework.visualization-type: pyramid or escalation ladder (wide/cheap at bottom, narrow/expensive at top)

levels.level: COUNT(*) source vs destination. Catches missing rows, duplicates, dropped partitions. Misses value drift within rows.
SUM(amount), MAX(updated_at), COUNT(DISTINCT id). Catches value drift that row count alone misses.
Hash every row, compare. Catches any difference at any granularity. Nuclear option -- reserve for critical tables or periodic audits.

framework.escalation-logic: Count mismatch triggers aggregate check. Aggregate mismatch triggers hash or full ID comparison.

framework.name: severity-calibration
framework.idea: Alert severity sits on two independent axes: what broke and how important the table is. The combination determines routing.

axis-1.name: condition-severity
axis-1.level: Destination data lost or significantly diverged
Load failed, destination stale, SLA breach
Anomaly detected but data is present and current
Nothing wrong -- log, dashboard, never notify

axis-2.name: table-importance
axis-2.example: Lookup dimension table being slightly stale

routing.rule: Critical condition on any table, or error on high-importance table
Warning on high-importance, error on low-importance
Info on anything, warning on low-importance

framework.name: load-strategy-spectrum
framework.idea: Load strategies ordered by cost and complexity. The corridor determines where on this spectrum each strategy becomes appropriate.

spectrum.position: Pure INSERT. No key matching. Near-zero DML cost. Idempotent at view level, not table level.
Rebuild from scratch (staging swap or partition swap). No merge logic. Idempotent by construction.
Append raw + dedup view. Shifts cost from load-time to read-time. Idempotent at view level.
Match on key, update or insert. Most expensive DML in columnar engines. Idempotent.
Two destinations (append log + merged current). Maximum complexity. "The upper bound of what's reasonable."

framework.corridor-note: In transactional-to-transactional, MERGE is cheap and native -- the spectrum compresses. In transactional-to-columnar, MERGE is the costliest operation and the spread matters.

framework.name: scheduling-maturity
framework.idea: Teams evolve through a predictable sequence of scheduling sophistication as pipeline count grows. Each stage is correct at a certain scale.
framework.visualization-type: maturity progression (three stages, left to right)

stages.stage: Everything runs together. Simple, works when you have a dozen tables and a daily window. Falls apart past ~4 hours or when intraday is needed.
Tables split by size or duration across time slots. Better throughput, but groupings don't map to business needs.
Tables grouped by downstream report/dashboard, scheduled to meet that consumer's freshness target. The destination.

framework.note: Don't over-engineer stage 3 when you have three dashboards.

framework.name: four-execution-points
framework.idea: Every conforming operation runs somewhere. The answer depends on who's idle. "Push work to whoever's idle."
framework.visualization-type: pipeline flow with four labeled processing points

points.point: Conform inside the SELECT. Free if source is off-hours. Dangerous on busy production OLTP.
Python, Spark, cloud function. Adds infrastructure and a data hop.
Land raw, transform in staging before final table. Processing cost on destination meter.
Transform inside the MERGE/INSERT. Works when transform is simple.

framework.heuristic: Source at 2am? Let it do the work. Production ERP at 10am? Extract raw, process downstream.

framework.name: schema-evolution-policy
framework.idea: When a new column arrives from the source, the pipeline responds per a declared policy. Only two policies are valid in ECL.
framework.visualization-type: decision gate or simple classification table

policies.policy: Accept new columns, add them to destination. Default for most tables.
Reject any schema change, fail the load. For critical stable-schema tables.
Drops rows with new columns. This is a transformation decision -- breaks the conforming boundary.
Drops the new column's values. Also breaks the conforming boundary.

framework.name: data-contract-taxonomy
framework.idea: A data contract covers four checkable aspects of the data, enforced at specific pipeline points.
framework.visualization-type: grid (4 contract types x 3 enforcement points)

contract-types.type: Expected column names and types. Evolve/freeze policy.
Expected row count range (min/max from recent history).
Expected null rates on key columns.

enforcement-points.point: Gate -- check before loading, block on violation.
Validation -- run checks after load completes.

framework.name: source-sensitivity-tiers
framework.idea: The type of source system determines how carefully you extract. Three tiers with different operational constraints.

tiers.tier: Live transactional system serving users. Maximum care. Off-peak scheduling, conservative concurrency, explicit query timeouts. You cannot request indexes or schema changes.
Same data, dedicated read connection. Same extraction patterns, wider tolerances. More concurrent queries, wider safe-hours windows.
No schema changes possible, limited query optimization, accept slower extractions. Work within vendor constraints. Often has its own backup/batch windows you must avoid.

framework.name: morning-routine-triage
framework.idea: Four items, fixed order, every morning. Everything else is drill-down from one of these.
framework.visualization-type: numbered checklist or four-quadrant dashboard mockup

sequence.item: Which tables are stale beyond their SLA
Any row count anomalies (spikes, drops, reconciliation deltas above threshold)

framework.principle: If the aggregate doesn't trigger curiosity, the individual tables are fine. Drill down only when something looks off.

framework.name: backfill-type-taxonomy
framework.idea: Three types of backfill, each with different scope and state-reset implications. Source pattern is in outline status -- taxonomy may evolve.

types.type: Reload a specific date range using partition swap or rolling window.
Reload the entire table. Reset all cursor state.
Reload specific records by primary key. Surgical fix.
